retrieved_docs.title: Commotio Cordis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanical force resulting from the precordial impact causes a stretch in myocardial cell membranes, which likely activates ion channels (mechanical-electrical coupling). [8] If the right number of these channels are in a vulnerable period of repolarization, the result of the depolarization may be ventricular fibrillation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Atrial Fibrillation -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most commonly, hypertension, structural, valvular, and ischemic heart disease illicit the paroxysmal and persistent forms of atrial fibrillation, but the underlying pathophysiology is not well understood. Some research has shown evidence of genetic causes of atrial fibrillation involving chromosome 10 (10q22-q24) that consists of a mutation in the gene alpha-subunit of the cardiac Ik5, which is responsible for pore formation. This is a gain of function mutation, allowing for more pores, increasing the activity within the ion channels of the heart, and thus affecting the stability of the membrane and reducing its refractory time. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Left Atrial Enlargement -- Pathophysiology -- Left Atrial Electrical Remodeling
retrieved_docs.content: Atrial tachyarrhythmias can be induced by triggers that generate ectopic activities or by substrate modifiers that promote reentry phenomena. Such modifiers are diverse, though many converge on common cellular pathophysiological mechanisms, such as shortened refractory periods, reduced actional potential duration, or altered atrial contractility.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrophysiologic Testing -- Technique or Treatment -- Measurements and stimulation protocols
retrieved_docs.content: Atrial extra-stimulus testing consists of a drive train of 8 paced beats at a constant cycle length (600 and 400ms) followed by an extra-stimulus delivered on the same catheter, on the same site. The drive train and the extra stimulus with a progressive decrescent value are repeating until there is a loss of atrial capture. The atrial extra stimulus testing serves for evaluation of the anterograde conduction over the AVN, atrial refractoriness, and induction of specific arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: Reentry Arrhythmia(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanic and electric properties of the cell are interdependent, which is called the mechanoelectrical feedback (MEF) or electrochemical coupling (ECC). Acute stretch induces cell currents causing depolarization (stretch-activated currents, SAC). [4] Regarding the physiologic mechanism, an acute stretch during systole shortens the action potential and the refractory period, and thus the wavelength which in general favors reentry. Chronic stretch leads to fibrosis and subsequently to different conduction across longitudinal and perpendicular cell axis, which is called anisotropy and leads to zig-zag conduction across cells. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Keshan Disease -- Pathophysiology -- Genetic Mutations Involved in Keshan Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Another study investigated the human SCN5A gene, which encodes a sodium channel within the cardiomyocyte membrane. This study revealed a significant association between the H558R polymorphism in exon 12 of SCN5A and Keshan disease. This genetic variation was significantly associated with increased susceptibility to Keshan disease and demonstrated potential connections with QRS prolongation observed in electrocardiogram (ECG) measurements. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Junctional Rhythm -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Autonomic and reflex causes: Vasovagal stimulation (eg, endotracheal suctioning), carotid sinus hypersensitivity

retrieved_docs.title: Reentry Arrhythmia(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of reentry is diverse and includes: Electrolyte abnormalities and channelopathies form a substrate for reentry arrhythmias. A mechanical force such as the chest thump maneuver induces but also terminates reentry. [4] Chronic cardiac stretch leads to remodeling and fibrosis that changes electrical properties of the myocardiocytes. Scar tissue following infarction is the basis of anatomical reentry. Congenital heart disease and surgery performed to treat it can cause reentry. Genetic culprits are related reentry tachycardias. Roberts et al. identified genetic culprits SCN5A and LMNA as a cause of idiopathic bundle branch reentry ventricular tachycardia. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Suspected Cardiac Arrhythmia -- Issues of Concern -- Palpitation
retrieved_docs.content: How arrhythmias produce changes in the strength of contraction is related to abrupt changes in the rhythm's cycle length, the interval between beats. The Frank-Starling law of the heart or the length-tension relationship states that when the interval between two beats lengthens, the force of contraction of the second beat will be greater, leading to its higher developed pressure and stroke volume. [3] Cycle-lengthening permits greater left-ventricular filling and increased myofiber stretch, producing greater actin-myosin overlap and eventually leading to increased LV pressure and stroke volume.

retrieved_docs.content: induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of ventricular tachycardia target the intrinsic accelerated ventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل) and its subtype ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني). Understanding these interconnected entities—electrical conduction abnormalities, structural cardiac changes, specific arrhythmic syndromes, diagnostic measurements, and therapeutic interventions—is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management of patients with cardiac rhythm disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 5 -- Community 2946
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy), indicating that structural changes in the left ventricle often accompany abnormal heart rhythms. In addition, cardiac dysrhythmias frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية (atrial flutter), reflecting a common overlap of rhythm disturbances that can influence diagnosis, management, and prognosis in patients with cardiac electrical abnormalities. Recognizing the link between cardiac dysrhythmias, left ventricular hypertrophy, and atrial flutter is important for comprehensive cardiac assessment and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2870
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias are classified under the broader category تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ and frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية. Recognizing that cardiac dysrhythmias belong to the supraventricular tachycardia family and are often associated with atrial flutter helps clinicians anticipate related rhythm disturbances, guide diagnostic evaluation, and tailor therapeutic strategies for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2833
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias, which are classified as a subcategory of تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ (supraventricular tachycardia), are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy). This connection suggests that individuals experiencing cardiac dysrhythmias often show structural changes in the left ventricle, underscoring the need to assess ventricular hypertrophy when diagnosing and treating supraventricular tachyarrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2746
retrieved_docs.content: CARDIAC CONDUCTION DISEASE WITH OR WITHOUT DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY commonly manifests as cardiac dysrhythmias, and these cardiac dysrhythmias are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy). This interconnection underscores the link between conduction system abnormalities, arrhythmic events, and structural remodeling of the left ventricle, which is important for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and therapeutic planning in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2747
retrieved_docs.content: Congestive cardiomyopathy (اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني) is clinically associated with both supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) and atrial flutter (رفرفة أذينية). These arrhythmic conditions frequently arise in the setting of congestive cardiomyopathy, reflecting a close pathophysiological link in which impaired myocardial function predisposes patients to rapid supraventricular rhythms and organized atrial flutter circuits. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as the presence of supraventricular tachycardia or atrial flutter may signal underlying congestive cardiomyopathy and guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at both the structural heart disease and its rhythm complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: non‑infective myocarditis/pericarditis (التهاب عضل القلب/التهاب التامور غير المعدي (SMQ)), acute anterior‑wall myocardial infarction, rheumatic heart disease, and non‑hypertensive congestive heart failure, often in the context of connective‑tissue disorders (اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة) and systemic lupus erythematosus. Electrophysiologic evaluation—arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of atrial fibrillation, supraventricular tachycardia, or ventricular tachycardia—is performed in cardiac electrophysiology studies and targets mechanisms such as ventricular acceleration. Pharmacologic therapy includes adenosine monophosphate for antidromic tachycardia (contra‑indicated in unspecified cardiac block) and verapamil, a phenylalkylamine calcium‑channel blocker, for supraventricular tachycardia. Implantation of a transvenous lead creates a cardiac pacemaker (kardiostimulátor) for intraventricular pacing in postoperative complete heart block and other conduction

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2350
retrieved_docs.content: Electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation is classified as a cardiac electrophysiological study (دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية). This approach combines the deliberate provocation of arrhythmias, precise identification and ablation of the arrhythmogenic focus, and therapeutic intervention for atrial fibrillation, thereby providing both diagnostic insight into the heart’s electrical behavior and a means to correct abnormal rhythms within a single procedural framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1526
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmics, class Ib are a group of sodium‑channel–blocking agents that preferentially affect depolarized cardiac tissue, and mexiletin is a member of this class. As a class Ib antiarrhythmic, mexiletin is employed in the management of ventricular arrhythmias and other rhythm disturbances, reflecting the pharmacologic properties shared by drugs within the Antiarrhythmics, class Ib category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2557
retrieved_docs.content: The electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation and the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia are both classified as a دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية (cardiac electrophysiological study). Each procedure involves deliberately inducing arrhythmias, identifying the arrhythmogenic focus, performing ablation of that focus, and delivering therapeutic treatment targeted to a specific rhythm disorder—atrial fibrillation in the first case and supraventricular tachycardia in the second. As forms of cardiac electrophysiological study, they provide essential diagnostic insight and therapeutic options for managing abnormal heart rhythms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 689

Overall, the interconnections among transient cerebral ischemia, its transitory clinical course, cardiac electrophysiology studies, specific arrhythmic foci (atrial fibrillation and ventricular acceleration), associated diseases (rheumatic heart disease, transient ischemic attack), and medication contraindications highlight a comprehensive framework for understanding the diagnosis, evaluation, and management of related cardiovascular and cerebrovascular conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 607
retrieved_docs.content: Phenylalkylamine derivatives, described as selective calcium channel blockers with direct cardiac effects, constitute a subclass of selective calcium channel blockers that exert direct actions on the heart. Verapamil is a member of this phenylalkylamine derivative class, inheriting the characteristic ability to selectively inhibit L‑type calcium channels and produce negative inotropic and chronotropic effects. Consequently, agents within this subclass, including verapamil, are employed in the treatment of conditions such as angina, hypertension, and certain cardiac arrhythmias, where modulation of cardiac contractility and conduction is therapeutically beneficial.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 924
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac electrophysiology study (دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية) is closely linked to conduction disorder (اضطراب التوصيل). Electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of atrial fibrillation, as well as the analogous evaluations for supraventricular tachycardia and ventricular tachycardia, are each classified as types of cardiac electrophysiology study. ECG monitors for arrhythmia (ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia) and ECG recordings that show a non‑sinus rhythm (ECG: non-sinus rhythm) both have a direct relationship with, and focus on, conduction disorder. Ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني) is a subcategory of an intrinsic accelerated ventricular system (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل) and serves as the primary focus of the electrophysiologic evaluation and ablation treatment for ventricular tachycardia. Together, these concepts illustrate how electrophysiologic studies, ECG monitoring, and specific arrhythmic mechanisms are interrelated in the assessment and management of cardiac conduction abnormalities and ventricular acceleration disorders.
